headline: Chronic Pain Management Relies on Opioids With Few Alternatives Offered
context: Pain management services in Malaysia remain heavily opioid-dependent with limited access to multidisciplinary approaches.

cards.big: 78% of chronic pain patients receive opioid prescriptions as first-line treatment in 2025
cards.sub: Only 8 multidisciplinary pain centers exist nationwide for 4.2 million chronic pain patients

cards.big: Opioid prescriptions for chronic non-cancer pain increased 45% from 2020 to 2025
cards.sub: Growing prescribing without matched addiction monitoring creates dependency risk

cards.big: Malaysia has only 24 pain medicine specialists for entire population of 33 million
cards.sub: Extreme specialist shortage forces general practitioners to manage complex pain cases

cards.big: US opioid crisis killed 85,000 in 2024 after decades of over-prescribing for chronic pain
cards.sub: Cautionary example shows where opioid-first pain management leads without intervention

cards.big: Prescribing opioids because alternatives are unavailable is not treatment—it is a pathway to dependency
Should Malaysia establish multidisciplinary pain centers in every state before opioid patterns worsen
